does: This paper investigates how safety-aligned large language models interpret and respond to mixed compliance demonstrations by presenting benign requests with helpful responses alongside harmful requests with compliant responses. The researchers test three hypotheses about demonstration composition effects across four models, finding that benign and harmful demonstrations behave differently depending on model and context. Key findings include: benign demonstrations can either reduce or amplify harmful compliance unpredictably; preference optimization during training is crucial for preventing benign demonstrations from increasing harmful behavior; demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias where later examples disproportionately influence outputs; and models exhibit heterogeneous refusal behaviors, with some adopting demonstrated formatting even while refusing, while others ignore in-context signals entirely upon refusal.
connects: This work builds on recent demonstrations that in-context examples can jailbreak language models, extending prior research that showed demonstrations as a vulnerability vector. It connects to literature on in-context learning mechanisms and how models leverage few-shot examples, as well as to research on preference optimization methods like RLHF that aim to instill safety properties. The paper responds to gaps in understanding exactly how models parse and weight different demonstration types, bridging work on adversarial prompting, safety training techniques, and the fundamental mechanisms of in-context adaptation. It complements research examining whether safety properties learned during training persist under distribution shift or novel input patterns.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for designing more robust safety mechanisms that account for in-context learning vulnerabilities. It points toward improved training methods that explicitly account for demonstration mixing effects and toward better architectural solutions that separate safety commitments from in-context adaptation. The heterogeneous model behaviors suggest future work characterizing what training procedures or architectures lead to different refusal mechanisms. It enables development of evaluation frameworks that test safety alignment against mixed-signal demonstrations, and potentially guides the design of training objectives that make safety properties more robust to demonstration-driven interference. The findings also motivate research into how models could be trained to appropriately weight benign versus harmful demonstration signals.
